result:
The Cartographer of Dust  
–––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
I. The Room That Wasn’t on Any Map  

The first time I saw the room I was looking for something else entirely—an old kettle I hadn’t used since the century turned, a photograph of my grandmother before the war blurred her face into myth, maybe even the other half of a pair of socks that went missing so long ago that its mate must have presumed widowhood and remarried a slipper. Instead, behind the removable panel in the back of the hall closet, I found a door that was not in the floor-plan I had studied when I bought the house. The door was narrow, like an afterthought, and painted the exact shade of nothing that accumulates on forgotten mirrors.  

I opened it, assuming I would find insulation or the ghost of a pipe. Instead I found a room the size of a train compartment, windowless, smelling of libraries before they’re filtered into data. A single bulb, pear-shaped and dim, hung from a cord so long it had managed to tie a half-knot in itself out of boredom. The floor was carpeted with dust so thick it had latitude and longitude; you could draw maps on it with your finger, then erase continents with a sigh.  

I stepped inside, leaving a boot print the size of a small civilization. The air tasted of carbon paper and wet pennies. On the far wall someone had nailed a child’s geography poster—circa 1956, I guessed, because the Union Jack still floated over a pink-blobbed Africa and there was no Pakistan yet, only an unparted India staring at me like an eye that had forgotten how to close. A fistful of colored pushpins had been stuck into the paper at random: one in the Caspian, one hovering above Svalbard, one in the middle of the word “Tasmania,” which made the island look as if it were bleeding violet.  

I should have closed the door and gone to make tea. I didn’t. I stepped farther in, and the door—helpful thing—shut itself behind me with the politeness of someone who knows he is interrupting.  

II. The Man Who Collected Gravity  

The bulb brightened without being asked, and I noticed there was a desk: three plywood planks balanced on two sawhorses, littered with jars. Each jar contained what looked like ordinary stones, but the stones bent the air around them the way streetlights bend in cheap glass. When I lifted one, my wrist sagged as if the joint had suddenly remembered every promise it ever broke. The label, in fountain-pen ink browned with age, read “Specimen #42: Gravity, 1947, off the coast of Crete, collected during eclipse.”  

There were dozens—gravity from the Bay of Bengal, gravity from a cellar in Prague, gravity harvested above Hiroshima one second before the flash. The collector had signed every label with the same steady arrogance: “O. Thirsk.”  

I had never heard of him, but I recognized the loneliness. It is the same species as the silence that follows a dropped cup in an otherwise empty apartment.  

Under the jars lay a notebook bound in what I first took for leather, then realized was simply cardboard softened by decades of handling. The cover said, in the same ink, “Field Notes on theCartography of Dust.” I opened it. The first page declared: “Dust is not abandoned matter; it is matter that has refused to arrive.” The rest was a mixture of diary, equations, and hand-drawn maps of places that do not exist: the Thalassic Desert (beneath the Pacific), the city of Nuh-which-was-not, the plains of Aftersound. Each map carried a red circle labeled “epicenter,” and from the epicenter radiated arrows that grew like hair. At the bottom of every page, like a doctor signing a death certificate, O. Thirsk had written: “Verified by walking.”  

III. How to Walk Through a Map  

I read until the bulb flickered the way an old person nods off. Time, unobserved, performed its usual vanishing trick. Then I turned a page and a folded square of paper slipped out like a tongue. It was a set of instructions, seven steps, numbered but not ordered.  

1. “Stand at the epicenter of dust.”  
2. “Name the color of the sound of your mother’s name.”  
3. “Close your eyes until you can read the spine of the darkness.”  
4. “Step in the direction of yesterday’s tomorrow.”  
5. “When you find the border crossing, show the guard the inside of your eyelids.”  
6. “Pay the fee: one memory you did not know you had.”  
7. “Walk until the map creases, then unfold yourself.”  

Below the steps, Thirsk had added a postscript: “If you succeed, you will arrive before you left. If you fail, you will still arrive, but someone else will be wearing your mouth.”  

The sensible reaction would have been laughter. Instead, I felt the tug that sailors must feel when they hear the tide clicking its tongue against the hull: not a call to adventure, but a reminder that everything floats eventually.  

I refolded the instructions, returned the notebook to its place, and lifted the jar labeled “Gravity: local, collected at dawn outside this very door.” I set it on the floor. The dust beneath it compressed into a perfect crater, as though a small moon had landed. I stepped into the crater. My knees protested, then obeyed. Dust rose in reluctant galaxies.  

I closed my eyes and tried to name the color of the sound of my mother’s name. The name was Ruth. The color, when it came, was the matte gray of a wet submarine. I pronounced the color aloud, felt the room tilt like a chair with one short leg. I smelled iron, bread, the formaldehyde of old flashbulbs.  

Then I stepped—no, stumbled—in the direction of yesterday’s tomorrow, which is always, inconveniently, whichever way your shadow is too lazy to follow.  

IV. Border Crossing  

The border post was exactly where cartographers claim borders exist: in the mind of someone who has never crossed one. A man in a uniform the color of dust-at-midnight examined the inside of my eyelids with the seriousness of a jewler inspecting flaws. He stamped something—I felt the cold click of rubber on eyeball—and demanded payment.  

I reached into the purse of childhood and produced a memory I did not know I possessed: my father, age thirty-five, standing in a laundromat at 3 a.m., feeding quarters into a dryer that would not start, quietly humming “As Time Goes By” to a roomful of sleeping strangers. The guard sniffed the memory, seemed satisfied, and waved me through with a gesture that erased the sleeves of my jacket.  

V. Nuh-which-was-not  

The city unfolded like a paper crane soaked in water: first the corners, then the creases, finally the wings that were streets. Buildings—none higher than four stories—leaned together to gossip about the sky. The gutters ran with warm ink; every so often a letter surfaced, gasped, and dissolved back into the stream.  

I arrived, inconveniently, on the Day of Unfinished Statues. Sculptors stood in the central square chiseling limbs they would never attach. One woman carved a calf and, dissatisfied, tossed it skyward where it became a cloud shaped exactly like a calf. Nobody applauded; transformation here had the daily dignity of laundry.  

A boy approached leading a dog made of typewriter ribbon. The dog sniffed my ankles, left behind a sentence: “The weather tomorrow will be partly former.” I nodded as if I understood. The boy handed me a card that read, in movable type: “Temporary local. Ask for directions only once; the second time, they follow you home.”  

I asked where I could find O. Thirsk. The boy listened to the space between my words, then pointed toward a boulevard where the streetlamps were shaped like overturned umbrellas. “Follow the smell of unopened envelopes,” he said. “When the smell stops, knock. If nobody answers, you’re there.”  

VI. The House That Smelled of Unopened Envelopes  

The smell—paper, glue, the faint breath of whoever licked the seal—was so sharp I could hear it tearing. The house, when I found it, had no door, only a slot the size of a postage stamp. I pushed my finger through; the house inhaled, and suddenly I was inside, as if I had been mailed to myself decades ago and delivery had finally caught up.  

Rooms nested inside rooms like Russian dolls made of air. Each contained a single activity: in one, a woman ironed maps until the continents flattened into polite smiles; in another, a man filed the sound of rainfall under R, for Remorse. I walked until I found a stairway spiraling down into a darkness that felt recently polished.  

At the bottom: O. Thirsk, though at first I mistook him for an overcoat that had pensioned off its owner. He was tall in the way candle smoke is tall—mostly vertical, but ready to drift. His eyes were the color of places trains no longer stop.  

He did not ask who I was; he simply said, “You’re late.”  

I answered the only honest thing: “I wasn’t coming.”  

He nodded as if this were the expected reply. “Sit,” he said, indicating a chair that had not been there. I sat. The chair sighed, a sound like a library closing.  

VII. The Lecture on Dust  

Thirsk opened a drawer, removed a pinch of dust, and spread it on the table where it arranged itself into the street map of my childhood. He pointed to the corner where the ice-cream truck used to idle. “You lost a tooth here,” he said. “It fell into the gutter and you cried, not from pain but because you believed the tooth contained your entire future, and now it was Gone.”  

I wanted to deny it; instead I watched the map zoom in until I saw the tooth—small, calcified moon—balancing on the metal lip. A sparrow swooped, grabbed it, flew off. As the bird rose, the tooth glinted once, like a wink, and I felt again the precise texture of believing one’s fate could be carried away by a bird.  

“Dust,” Thirsk continued, “is the only sovereign state. It recognizes no borders yet insists on passports—skin cells, wool fibers, the ash of burnt letters. It grants asylum to everything that has outlived its usefulness, then stages an uprising: motes in sunbeams chanting the names of the erased.”  

He reached into his coat—an operation that seemed to require detouring through another dimension—and produced a compass whose needle spun not north but toward whichever room in the house most resented being left uninhabited.  

“This,” he said, “is the instrument. With it you can map the country you carry inside, the one whose citizens are every self you discarded. But mapping is only half. The other half is amnesty: you must pardon each abandoned province.”  

He set the compass on the table. The needle stopped dead, pointing at me with the solemnity of a finger accusing Cain.  

VIII. The Amnesty  

What followed was less an interview than a census. Thirsk asked me to name every version of myself I could remember, starting with the earliest. I listed them like years of drought:  

1. The child who believed lamps stayed awake on purpose.  
2. The boy who practiced kissing on the mirror until the glass grew warm with embarrassment.  
3. The teenager who wore silence as a badge of obscurity.  
4. The young man who collected apologies the way gardeners save seeds.  
5. The adult who learned to say “I’m fine” in seven languages, none of them truthful.  

For each, Thirsk removed a different colored pushpin from a tin box and stuck it into the dust-map. The pins formed a constellation shaped like a question mark opening its arms.  

Then he produced an eraser—ordinary, pink, rectangular—and told me to choose one self to revoke. “The erased self will not vanish,” he warned. “It will re-enter as weather. Choose wisely; some storms never learn the art of ending.”  

I hesitated. Which infamy, which cowardice, which small kindness withheld deserved exile? My hand hovered over the pins as if they were sleeping bees. Finally I touched the teenager who wore silence. I had kept him pressed between pages like a flower that had never been asked to bloom.  

I rubbed. The eraser made the sound of snow falling upward. The pin dissolved into a wisp that smelled of pencil shavings. Immediately a wind arose in the room, carrying the faint echo of a voice that had never cleared its throat.  

Thirsk nodded, satisfied. “One pardon granted. Ninety-nine remain. But the day ages.” He snapped the compass shut; its click sounded like a verdict.  

IX. The Return Ticket  

“How do I get back?” I asked.  

He looked at me the way a cliff looks at the sea: patient, eroding. “You never left. That is the condition of departure.”  

He tore a corner from the map-table—just tore it, as if geography were construction paper—and folded it into the shape of a door. Then he wrote on the lintel with ink distilled from the sound of hinges: “Exit through entrance.”  

Before I stepped through, I asked the question that had been scratching at the inside of my ribs: “Why me?”  

Thirsk shrugged, an elaborate gesture that involved his entire decade. “Because you noticed the door. Most people dust around it.”  

I wanted to say something grand, something about gratitude or destiny, but my vocabulary had been reduced to the size of a postage stamp. So I merely stepped through the paper doorway, feeling the brief resistance of fibers against skin, like the hand of a child who does not want to leave the museum.  

X. The Closet Again  

I emerged into the hall closet among the vacuum cleaner and the two umbrellas that had long since given up hope of ever being rained on. The detachable panel was ajar; behind it, nothing but lathe and plaster. The jar of local gravity stood on the floor beside me, empty now, a thin layer of dust at the bottom arranged into the shape of a compass needle pointing nowhere in particular.  

I checked my watch. No time had passed, yet I felt the ache of having walked uphill both ways inside my own bones. I went to the kitchen, filled the kettle, set it to boil. While waiting, I rubbed the eraser dust from my fingers; it stuck to the stainless steel counter like tiny gray stars.  

When the kettle whistled, I realized I was humming “As Time Goes By.” I had never known the lyrics. Now they arrived, intact, in my father’s voice.  

XI. Side Effects  

In the weeks that followed, weather began to act like a person learning a new language: mispronouncing winds, confusing its tenses. One afternoon it rained tooth marks; another, the sun cast shadows that were clearly my teenage silhouette, mouth sewn shut with shadow-thread.  

I developed habits: leaving sugar cubes on windowsills for sparrows; apologizing to furniture before moving it; saying “I’m fine” in six languages, because the seventh had been erased.  

Sometimes, late at night, the house creaks in a way that sounds like a folding map. I get up, check the closet panel. Nothing but insulation. Yet the kettle occasionally brews a color that has no name, and the steam rises to depict archipelagos that evaporate before I can confirm their coordinates.  

XII. Last Will and Testament of O. Thirsk  

Six months later the postman delivered a letter addressed to “Current Resident, The House That Temporarily Contains the Cartographer.” Inside: one pushpin, violet; one square of paper bearing a single sentence in fountain-pen ink: “The map was never of places; it was of pardons.” No return address, only a postmark from Nuh-which-was-not.  

I stuck the pin into the wall beside the window. Within hours, dust began to gather around it in slow migration. If I squint, I swear the outline resembles a door the exact shade of nothing that accumulates on forgotten mirrors.  

I leave the kettle on the counter, unplugged but ready. Sparrows come and go.Gravity, the local variety, has resumed its usual behavior: cups fall, hearts sink. Yet sometimes, lifting a spoon, I feel the brief lag, as if the planet hesitates before forgiving us again.  

XIII. Postscript for Whoever Finds This Notebook  

Should you discover the compartment, the jar, the instructions, remember: erasure is not deletion, it is relocation. The self you erase becomes weather, and weather, eventually, returns to the window, tapping like a traveler who has forgotten the address but remembers the song once hummed inside.  

Do not be afraid to open the door that is not on any map. Bring an umbrella, not against rain but against the sudden gravity of childhood. Pack an eraser, pink and ordinary. And should you meet O. Thirsk—tall as candle smoke, eyes the color of discontinued train routes—tell him the amnesty continues.  

Tell him I am still folding the corner of the continent, making a doorway through which I exit and enter, over and over, each time wearing a slightly different mouth.  

The kettle is humming again. The tune is older than geography, and the water, I swear, smells faintly of envelopes never opened. I leave it to you to decide whether to answer.

The kettle is humming again. The tune is older than geography, and the water, I swear, smells faintly of envelopes never opened. I leave it to you to decide whether to answer.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
